The difficulties David faced prepared him for future advancement. This is how the Lord deals with those he prepares for glory. He does not immediately give them possession of the kingdom, but leads them through much suffering, which he uses to fit them for it. They should not complain about his gracious plan, nor doubt his care; instead, they should look forward with joyful expectation to the crown that is reserved for them. Sometimes it seems there is only a step between us and death; at all times it may be so, and we should prepare for it. But even when dangers seem most threatening, we cannot die until God’s purpose concerning us is accomplished; nor until we have served our generation according to his will, if we are believers. Jonathan generously offers David his services. This is true friendship. Christ shows his love to us by saying, Ask, and it will be done for you; and we must show our love to him by keeping his commandments.

Jonathan faithfully promises that he will let David know how his father feels towards him. It will benefit ourselves and our families to secure a relationship with those whom God favors, and to make his friends ours. True friendship rests on a firm foundation and is able to silence ambition, self-love, and undue regard for others. But who can fully understand the love of Jesus, who gave himself as a sacrifice for rebellious, polluted sinners! How great then ought to be the strength and effects of our love to him, to his cause, and his people!

None were more constant than David in attending religious duties; nor would he have been absent, but self-preservation obliged him to withdraw. In times of great danger, opportunities for divine services may be missed. But it is bad for us, except in cases of necessity, to omit any opportunity of regularly attending them. Jonathan wisely and well secured an interest in David, yet for this he is blamed. It is good to take God’s people as our people. It will prove advantageous to us at last, however it may now be thought against our interests. Saul was furious. What savage beasts, and worse, does anger make men!

The separation of two such faithful friends was painful for both, but David's situation was more regrettable, because David was leaving all his comforts, even those of God’s sanctuary. Christians need not grieve as people without hope; but being one with Christ, they are one with each other, and will meet in his presence soon, to part no more; to meet where all tears shall be wiped from their eyes.
